(6 comments)

The patch itself looks reasonable, but I don't think it could be landed until there is the ability to convert existing inode quotas so use this new method.  Mark -1 for now until the follow-on patch is available and for minor cleanups/improvements.

Patch Set 3:

(1 comment)

"The patch itself looks reasonable, but I don't think it could be landed until there is the ability to convert existing inode quotas so use this new method. Mark -1 for now until the follow-on patch is available and for minor cleanups/improvements."

The dnode accounting tool is not part of this patch, or a patch on Lustre side. It will be a ZFS tool depending on patch 15180, which will scan existing dnodes and check if it's created before dnode accounting feature is enabled.

32b consistently fails with or w/o this patch on zfs backend - working on it.

Patch Set 18:

> 32b consistently fails with or w/o this patch on zfs backend -
 > working on it.

It turned out that the disk image used by test script is an old one that doesn't support native dnode accounting. When new code is running on that old disk image, it will just estimate the dnode count, which has a discrepancy with the actual number.

It seems the correct way to fix this problem would be to create a new disk image with new code. However, should I do that and commit a new disk image?

commit.subject: LU-2435 osd-zfs: use zfs native dnode accounting
commit.message:
LU-2435 osd-zfs: use zfs native dnode accounting

If ZFS userobj_accounting feature exists, use it for osd-zfs object
accounting; otherwise estimate object use by block space.

Disable quota check in conf-sanity:32b and sanity-quota:7e if the
underlying zfs version is below 0.7.0. For conf-sanity test, if the
running ZFS is version 0.7.0 or higher, upgrade the zpool image to
enable native dnode accounting. That ensures we also test the upgrade
procedure.

Test-Parameters: mdtfilesystemtype=zfs ostfilesystemtype=zfs testlist=sanity-quota,sanity-quota,sanity-quota,conf-sanity,conf-sanity envdefinitions=SLOW=yes
